en: If you want to dramatically improve your level of speaking and writing in English
language, stay with me.
Hello, this is Matti from POC English and in this lesson I want to talk about
something very important. The reason why many students think that their English
is not good is because they don't use good words or good adjectives. They often
stick to the simple adjectives like good, bad, nice, happy, sad or simple words
look, walk or like. One way to improve your level of written and spoken English
is by learning some alternatives to simple words and simple adjectives. In
this lesson that's what we're going to do. First, let's start with simple
adjectives and number one is nice. We can use the adjective nice for nearly
everything. A nice camera, a nice microphone, a nice laptop, nice room, nice
person. Look at this sentence. We had a nice evening. Here nice means enjoyable
so instead of nice you can say we had an enjoyable evening. You can also use
pleasurable. We had a pleasurable evening. So we had a nice evening, we had
an enjoyable evening or we had a pleasurable evening. We can use nice to
describe people like saying the host was really nice. Now here nice can mean
polite and with good manners so instead of nice we can use gracious and say the
host was very gracious or you can also say courteous. The host was courteous
gracious, courteous meaning polite. And look at this sentence. I went on a date
with a very nice guy. Now here nice means likable, someone I liked. I went on a
date with a very nice guy. Instead of nice you can say likable with a likable
guy or lovely with a lovely guy. A more formal and advanced way of saying nice
in this case is to say congenial, congenial. It means friendly. So I went on
a date with a congenial guy or with a congenial person to make it formal. Or
another adjective is cordial, cordial. Now listen up. It's not cordial in American
pronunciation. It's cordial, cordial. Cordial and congenial both mean friendly
and nice. So instead of nice, depending on the meaning of nice, you can say
enjoyable, pleasurable, gracious or courteous, likable, lovely, congenial or
cordial. Now adjective number two which is very much used in everyday
conversation and even in written text is good. Again we can use good for almost
everything. A good person, a good camera, a good room, a good computer, a good video.
You can say that restaurant is good. Now instead of good what else can we say? If
you want to say it's very good you can use excellent or amazing. An amazing
restaurant or an excellent restaurant. If it's very, very good you can say it's
wonderful or marvelous, wonderful, marvelous. And if it is very, very, very
good you can say it's exceptional, outstanding, splendid, stupendous. All
these adjectives mean very, very good. So you can say excellent, amazing,
wonderful, marvelous or exceptional, outstanding, splendid or stupendous
instead of good. And the opposite of good is bad. Again bad can be used for almost
everything. For example that is a bad restaurant. Now instead of bad what else
can you say? You can say awful. That is a bad restaurant. That is an awful
restaurant. That's an awful situation. You can say terrible which means very bad, a
terrible restaurant. You can say unpleasant which means it's not pleasant,
it's not good, it's bad, it's an unpleasant restaurant. Or you can say
lousy. In that restaurant the food was lousy or service was lousy. So in this
case lousy, awful, terrible or unpleasant mean bad. You can also use bad for
people and say, for example, we have a bad neighbor. Instead of bad in this case
you can use the adjective disagreeable. We have a disagreeable neighbor which
means a bad neighbor. If the person is unkind or unfriendly you can use the
mean. I have a very mean neighbor or dude stop being mean. If there is a
person with bad intentions, a person who wants to do something bad, you can say
they are wicked. Wicked which means they have very bad intentions. A wicked old
What else can we say? You can simply say I'm unhappy or I'm upset. If you are
really sad you can say I'm miserable or I'm depressed. If you are very, very sad
you can say I'm dejected. So I'm unhappy, I'm upset, I'm dejected, I'm depressed,
I'm miserable. Now sometimes there are things which make you feel sad. For
example, the news was sad. Here you can say depressing. The news was depressing.
You can also use two very interesting adjectives. You can say sorrowful. The
news was sorrowful. Or if it made you cry you can say it was tearful. The news was
tearful. And the opposite of sad is happy. I am so happy. Instead of happy you can
say cheerful. I'm very cheerful now. Or the kids were cheerful. You can say
delighted. Oh what an amazing surprise. I'm delighted. It means I'm very, very
happy. When you meet someone and you want to say I'm happy to meet you, you can
also use the word glad. Glad to meet you. Or you can also say pleased. Pleased to
meet you sir. Glad or pleased. And if you're really happy you can say you are
joyful or elated. Oh I'm elated. Okay we've learned some alternatives for five
very simple adjectives. Now it is time for verbs. But before we get to the verbs
if you want to receive the summary of every video I post on YouTube you can go
to POCEnglish.com. The link is in the description down below. Type in your
email address and click subscribe. I post two videos weekly and after posting each
video I will send you the PDF file and after posting each video you will
receive the PDF file of the summary of that video so you can review it. Don't
forget to check the descriptions down below. And now with the verbs. And verb
number one is like. It's something we use a lot. Even me I use it a lot. And listen
it's not wrong to say like all the time but I'm saying if you want to improve
your English a little bit you can learn some alternatives. We can use the verb
like for people. For example I like Tom Cruise. He's an amazing actor. Instead of
like here I can say admire. I admire Tom Cruise or I adore Tom Cruise. I can also
say respect. I respect Tom Cruise. And not just Tom Cruise. It can be used for
your teacher. I adore my teacher. I admire my teacher. I respect my teacher. If you
are attracted to someone you can say I like her or I like him. But instead of
like in this case you can also use the verb fancy. I fancy her or I fancy him.
Means I am attracted to him or I am attracted to her. You can also use fancy
in questions when you want to offer something. For example do you like a
drink? I can also say do you fancy a drink? Sometimes like means that
something is very valuable to you. For example this is my thesis. I recently
graduated from university and I worked so hard for it. This is of high value to
me. I like my thesis. I can also use the verb treasure and say I treasure my
thesis. Which means I think it's very valuable to me. I treasure it. I can also
say cherish. I cherish my thesis. It means it's of high value to me. You can
also use these two verbs for your loved ones like your mother, your father or
your family. I cherish my mother or I treasure my family. Verb number two is
walk. Now you might say why should I learn some alternatives to walk? Well I
walk. You walk. We all walk in the same way. But really do we? You can say I want
to go for a walk in the neighborhood. But if you want to go for a walk but you
don't have a specific destination, there is no fixed place you want to go to, you
can use the word stroll. Stroll. And instead of walk you can say I want to
stroll around the neighborhood. Which means to walk aimlessly but just for
pleasure. I want to stroll around. Let's have some fun. Let's see what's new in
the neighborhood. You can also use the verb wonder which means to walk
aimlessly. For example those guys are wandering around the town or they are
walking aimlessly. Now sometimes your leg is hurt and you can't walk properly. So
in this case it's like you go. What am I doing? Am I walking? Yes but a specific
word for it is limp. I'm limping. Oh my leg. I'm limping. In the middle of the
night I'm feeling hungry and I want to go to the kitchen, open the fridge and
have a little snack. But my parents are asleep and I don't want my mom to
notice it. So what do I do? I walk quietly on the tip of my toes. And the
verb is to tiptoe. I tiptoed to the kitchen to have a snack. It means I
walked quietly to the kitchen to have some snacks. Or sometimes you see someone
on the street who had a lot to drink. And how are they walking? They're not
walking in the proper way. They're just walking like this. Here you can say they
are staggering. I saw a guy staggering on the streets and he was about to fall. So
there are different ways of walking. Strolling, wandering, tiptoeing, limping or
staggering. And the last verb is look. Now you can say I looked at the painting
but if you looked at the painting for a long time you can use the verb gaze. I
gazed at the painting for hours. Or stare. The guy was staring at me. It means he
was looking at me without taking his eyes off me. If you take a quick look at
something you can use the verb glance. For example, I just glanced at my watch. I
took a quick look. Or let's see if I have any messages. No, I just glanced at my
phone. Or sometimes you're playing a game and you say cover your eyes and then you
cover your eyes but then you are peeking. To peek means to look at something but
secretly and without wanting others to know that you are looking. So I am
peeking and then you can say no peeking, no peeking and then I close my hand. So
instead of look you can say gaze or stare, glance or peek. And that's it guys.
If you like this video don't forget to give it a thumbs up and if you're new to my channel click subscribe.
